The Double Burden: Child Deprivation and Vaccine Coverage

A UNICEF Data Analysis Report

Author

Girish Rajendra Ghate

Published

April 15, 2026

Every child deserves a healthy start to life, yet millions around the world face multiple overlapping disadvantages — from lack of clean water and sanitation to inadequate nutrition and healthcare access.

This report examines two critical dimensions of child wellbeing using UNICEF data:

  1. Child deprivation — the percentage of children suffering from exactly five simultaneous deprivations
  2. Vaccine coverage — the percentage of infants receiving their first dose of inactivated polio vaccine (IPV1)

By combining these indicators with economic metadata such as GDP per capita and life expectancy, we uncover a troubling pattern: 13 countries face a “double burden” where children experience both high deprivation and low vaccine coverage — including Madagascar, Ethiopia, Chad, and Nigeria.

1 Exploratory Data Analysis

Before creating visualisations, I explored all three datasets to understand their structure, coverage, and data quality.

1.1 Key EDA Findings

  • Child deprivation data covers 72 countries across 2011–2018 with Male, Female, and Total breakdowns. Most values cluster near zero, with Madagascar (1.43%) and Ethiopia (1.22%) showing the highest deprivation rates. There are 0 missing values.
  • Vaccine coverage data covers 194 countries across 2015–2024. Coverage ranges from 0% to 99%, with significant regional variation.
  • Economic metadata spans 1960–2024 across 211 countries, but contains 2,494 missing values for GDP per capita and 245 missing values for life expectancy.
  • The indicator datasets use different year formats from the metadata, which required alignment before joining.
  • 72 countries appear in both indicator files, enabling cross-analysis of deprivation and vaccine coverage.

2 Data Preparation and Transformations

To build a comprehensive analytical dataset, I performed three key merges:

  1. Joined the child deprivation data (72 countries) with economic metadata to add GDP per capita and life expectancy — producing a merged dataset of 72 rows across 17 columns.
  2. Joined the vaccine coverage data (194 countries) with the same economic metadata — producing 1,823 rows across 18 columns.
  3. Merged both indicators together across their overlapping period (2015–2018) to identify 19 double-burden countries where children face both high deprivation (>0.1%) and low vaccine coverage (<70%).

I used left joins to preserve all indicator observations, even for countries where economic data was unavailable. Year formats were aligned across datasets to ensure accurate matching.

3 Global Vaccine Coverage: A World Map

The map below reveals stark geographic inequalities in polio vaccine coverage. While most of Europe and the Americas achieve coverage above 90%, large parts of Sub-Saharan Africa lag behind, leaving millions of children vulnerable to preventable disease.

The map confirms a clear north-south divide. Countries like Central African Republic (39%), Papua New Guinea (42%), and Yemen (48%) have coverage below 50%, while Europe achieves near-universal coverage.

4 Regional Comparison: Vaccine Coverage by Continent

Africa has the lowest average coverage, falling well below the global 90% target. Europe leads with near-universal coverage. This gap mirrors broader patterns of healthcare investment.

5 The Economic Link: GDP and Vaccine Coverage

Is vaccine coverage simply a matter of national wealth? The scatterplot below examines this relationship with a linear regression line.

The regression line shows a positive correlation — wealthier nations tend to have higher coverage. However, several low-income countries achieve coverage above 80%, proving that policy choices and healthcare systems matter as much as raw wealth.

6 Trends Over Time: Double-Burden Countries

How has vaccine coverage evolved in nations facing the double burden of high child deprivation and low vaccine coverage?

Ethiopia shows the most dramatic improvement, climbing from ~30% to over 60%. Chad and Madagascar remain stubbornly low. The shaded COVID-19 period (2020–2021) shows visible dips in several countries, highlighting how pandemic disruptions set back routine immunisation.

7 Gender Disparity in Child Deprivation

Does deprivation affect boys and girls equally? Using the gender- disaggregated data in Indicator 1, I compare male and female rates across the most affected countries.

8 Conclusion

This analysis of UNICEF data reveals a troubling picture:

  1. Geographic concentration — Child deprivation and low vaccine coverage are both concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa, with Madagascar, Ethiopia, and Chad consistently among the worst affected.
  2. The double burden — 13 countries face both high deprivation (>0.1%) and low vaccine coverage (<70%). Children in these nations are denied even the most basic protections.
  3. Economic correlation, not determinism — Higher GDP correlates with better coverage, but several low-income countries outperform expectations, proving that policy choices matter.
  4. COVID-19 setbacks — The pandemic disrupted vaccine delivery in already vulnerable nations, and some have yet to recover.
  5. Gender equality in suffering — Deprivation affects boys and girls almost equally, suggesting the drivers are systemic rather than gender-specific.

These findings underscore the urgent need for targeted investment in the 13 double-burden countries, where children face overlapping vulnerabilities demanding an integrated response.
